topic: younger brother Yusuf acting up at school — mum is stressed

turns.content: I need to vent because my mum just called me in tears and now I can't concentrate on anything
I'm sorry to hear that — it's really hard to focus when someone you care about is upset. What happened?

my younger brother Yusuf has been proper acting up at school. like he got sent to the head teacher's office AGAIN and my mum got called in for a meeting. she's so stressed about it and when she's stressed she calls me because apparently I'm supposed to have all the answers
That's a lot of pressure on you — being the person your mum turns to when things go wrong. What's Yusuf been doing? Is it behavioural stuff, or academic, or a mix?

behavioural mainly. like disrupting class, being rude to teachers, not doing homework. classic acting out stuff. and the thing is he's not a bad kid at all — at home he's sweet and funny and normal. so something about school specifically is not working for him

That disconnect between how he is at home versus at school is actually really telling. If he's fine at home but acting out at school, it usually points to something specific in the school environment — boredom, social dynamics, a teacher he doesn't connect with, or even just feeling like he doesn't fit in.

Has anyone at the school tried to actually talk to him about what's going on, or are they just disciplining the behaviour without digging into the cause?

that's the thing that's frustrating me!! they just keep sending him out of class and calling my mum in like that's going to fix anything. nobody's actually sat down with him and been like what's going on with you. they just see a kid being disruptive and go straight to punishment

Yeah, that reactive approach rarely works — it just creates a cycle where the kid gets labelled as "the difficult one" and starts living up to that reputation. The school should be looking at why he's behaving this way, not just punishing the symptoms.

A few things that might help:

1. **Request an actual meeting** — not a "your son is in trouble" call, but a proper sit-down where your mum (or you, if she'd like support) discusses what's going on with his form tutor or head of year

2. **Ask if the school has a pastoral support team or a counsellor** — most schools do, and getting Yusuf referred to them could give him a space to talk

3. **Talk to Yusuf yourself** — sometimes a sibling who's a bit older but not a parent gets a more honest answer than mum or dad would

the talking to him myself bit is so true. he tells me things he'd never tell my mum. I think part of the problem is he's at that age where he's trying to figure out who he is and the boys at school are a bad influence tbh. like his friends are all about being the class clown and if you actually try in lessons you get called a neek

That peer pressure dynamic is brutal at that age. The social currency is being funny and not caring about school, and actually trying gets you labelled negatively. It takes a lot of confidence to go against that when you're young and your social standing feels like the most important thing in the world.

The fact that he opens up to you is really valuable. When you talk to him, try not to go in with the "you need to sort yourself out" angle — he'll shut down. Instead, ask him what's going on with his mates, what he actually thinks about school, what subjects he finds boring versus interesting. Make it a conversation, not a lecture.

yeah I know if I lecture him he'll just switch off. I was thinking of taking him out for food this weekend, just me and him, and having a proper chat. somewhere he feels relaxed not at home where mum is hovering loool

That's a really smart approach. Getting him out of the house and away from the family dynamic makes it feel less like an intervention and more like just hanging out. Food is always a good setting for real conversations — it gives you something to do with your hands and takes the pressure off direct eye contact.

Let him talk first. Ask open questions. And if he does open up about the peer pressure stuff, validate it — "yeah that sounds hard" goes a lot further than "well you need better friends" even if that's what you're thinking.

the hardest part is my mum though. she takes it so personally, like she's failed as a parent. and I'm trying to reassure her while also managing my own life and work and she doesn't fully get that I've got my own stress going on. I love her to death but sometimes being the eldest is exhausting wallah

That's a really heavy position to be in — mediating between your mum's distress and your brother's behaviour while carrying your own workload. You're basically functioning as a co-parent and a therapist on top of everything else you have going on.

It's okay to set gentle boundaries with your mum even while being supportive. Something like: "Mum, I'm going to talk to Yusuf this weekend and I'll help you figure out the school meeting, but I need you to know I've got a lot on my plate too." You don't have to fix everything in one phone call.

And honestly — kids act up. It doesn't mean she's failed. Yusuf is at a difficult age, he's navigating peer dynamics, and the school isn't handling it well. That's not her fault.

you're right. I think I just absorb everyone's stress and then wonder why I feel overwhelmed loool. okay I'm going to call Yusuf tonight and sort the weekend plan, and I'll tell mum to ask the school about pastoral support. that's actionable at least

That's a solid plan — two concrete actions instead of spiralling in worry. Talk to Yusuf in a relaxed setting this weekend, and get your mum to push the school for actual support rather than just repeated disciplinary meetings.

And genuinely — try to protect some time for yourself this week too. You can't pour from an empty cup, as the saying goes. Your mum and Yusuf both need you functioning well, which means you need to not take on the full weight of this.

Hope the chat with him goes well this weekend.
